categories.title: Interviews
categories.summary: Field interview recordings from our empathy research sessions.
categories.description: Original voice recordings captured during one-on-one empathy interviews conducted across different user segments. Each clip is paired with a written transcript in the Transcripts section.

blocks.heading: About These Interviews
paragraphs: We conducted structured empathy interviews with people from varied backgrounds to understand how they encounter and respond to misinformation online. Each conversation was kept conversational and open-ended, allowing interviewees to steer the discussion toward what mattered most to them.

The recordings here are unedited and unscripted — what you hear is exactly what was said. Some sessions run long because the most interesting observations tended to come up naturally, well past the initial questions. We found that giving people room to talk led to far richer data than sticking to a rigid script.
Select a person below to listen to their session. Written transcripts of every recording are available in the Transcripts section.

categories.title: Consolidated Personas
categories.summary: Merged personas synthesized from across our user research.
categories.description: After interviewing across user segments, we consolidated overlapping traits into a small set of representative personas.

blocks.heading: How We Built These
paragraphs: Individual personas were first created for each interviewee based on their specific goals, frustrations, tech habits, and media behaviour. Once we had all the raw personas in front of us, patterns started to surface pretty quickly.

We grouped them by shared motivations and pain points, then collapsed overlapping traits into three consolidated archetypes. Each consolidated persona is a composite drawn from multiple real people, not a fictional invention. The quotes and frustrations attributed to each are pulled directly from interview transcripts.

The three personas represent meaningfully different relationships with online information: one shares impulsively without verifying, one fact-checks obsessively but gets worn down by it, and one scrolls anxiously without any reliable way to know what to trust. These differences shaped every design decision that followed.

categories.title: Personas Comparison Chart
categories.summary: A side-by-side comparison of all personas across key dimensions.
categories.description: A chart mapping all personas against shared attributes to identify patterns and differences.

blocks.heading: What This Chart Shows
paragraphs: Once we had all individual personas documented, we needed a way to see them together rather than in isolation. This comparison chart maps every persona across the same set of dimensions: digital literacy, frequency of encountering misinformation, reaction type (share vs verify vs ignore), trust in media, and emotional response to false content.

The chart made it immediately clear that our interviewees were not a uniform group. The spread across dimensions is wide enough that a single solution targeting one type of user would fail the others. This insight directly influenced our decision to build something passive and ambient rather than something that demands active engagement from the user.

categories.title: HMW Question
categories.summary: Our refined How Might We question and how we arrived at it.
categories.description: The How Might We question that anchors our project and the reasoning behind its framing.

blocks.heading: Framing the Problem as Opportunity
paragraphs: How Might We questions sit at the hinge between research and ideation. They are specific enough to be actionable but open enough not to pre-suppose a solution. Getting the framing right took us several rounds of iteration.

Our first attempts were either too broad ("How might we stop misinformation?") or too narrow ("How might we add a warning to fake news?"). Neither worked. Broad questions generate unfocused ideas. Narrow ones lead you straight to a solution before you have understood the problem.

The shortlisted questions below represent our best attempts at the right level of specificity. The final HMW we landed on acknowledges the role of algorithmic amplification, keeps the user in the centre, and leaves room for a wide enough set of solutions to be useful in ideation.

items.caption: Top 10 HMW questions shortlisted by the team

categories.title: Journey Map
categories.summary: The end-to-end experience of our primary personas.
categories.description: A visualization of touchpoints, emotions, and friction points across the user journey.

blocks.heading: Mapping the Experience
paragraphs: We created journey maps for each of our three consolidated personas to trace what actually happens when they encounter potentially misleading content online. The maps cover the full arc: from the initial scroll and first encounter with suspect content, through their internal deliberation, to whatever action they take (share, ignore, verify, feel bad about it).

What the maps revealed was how much emotional weight each encounter carries. For the Impulsive Sharer, there is almost no friction point at all before sharing. For the Cautious Verifier, the journey is exhausting, requiring multiple tabs, cross-referencing, and still no certainty. For the Anxious Consumer, there is a persistent low-grade dread that never fully resolves.

These maps grounded our ideation. Any solution we built needed to interrupt the Impulsive Sharer before the share, support the Cautious Verifier without adding to their cognitive load, and give the Anxious Consumer something concrete to hold on to.

categories.title: Personas
categories.summary: Individual personas captured from raw interview data.
categories.description: Each persona represents one interviewee's goals, frustrations, and context, before consolidation.

blocks.heading: Raw Personas from Interviews
paragraphs: Before we consolidated, each interview produced its own persona card. These individual personas are grounded entirely in what one specific person told us during their session. No merging, no averaging, no assumptions filled in.

The value of keeping the individual personas alongside the consolidated ones is traceability. When a design decision later gets challenged, we can always go back and point to a specific person who articulated exactly this need or exactly this frustration. The individual personas are evidence; the consolidated ones are synthesis.
Select a person below to view the persona cards created from their interviews.

categories.title: RCA
categories.summary: Root cause analysis behind the problem we chose.
categories.description: A structured breakdown of the underlying causes that produce the symptoms our users report.

blocks.heading: Why Root Cause Analysis?
paragraphs: Every frustration and confusion our interviewees described was a symptom. RCA is the process of tracing those symptoms back to their actual origins rather than designing against the surface complaint.

Our interviewees kept landing on the same visible problem: they were not sure what to believe when scrolling through their feeds. But the causes behind that uncertainty are not a single thing. They include how recommendation algorithms optimise for engagement over accuracy, how platform interfaces de-emphasise source credibility, how social proof (likes, shares) gets conflated with factual reliability, and how the sheer velocity of content makes slowing down to verify feel like an impossible ask.

The RCA diagram below traces these causes across four layers: individual behaviour, platform design, algorithmic incentives, and information ecosystem dynamics. Understanding all four layers was essential to framing an HMW question that was genuinely solvable rather than just gesturing at a big systemic problem.

categories.title: Responses and Graph (HMW)
categories.summary: Quantitative responses to our HMW survey, visualized.
categories.description: Raw response counts and visualizations from the survey we ran to validate the HMW question.

blocks.heading: About the Survey
paragraphs: Before committing to a single HMW question, we ran a quick survey to check whether the problem we were framing resonated with a wider group beyond our interviewees. The survey presented multiple candidate HMW questions and asked respondents to rate each one on relevance and urgency.

The responses confirmed that questions framed around algorithmic amplification and real-time browsing resonated more strongly than questions focused on media literacy education or post-hoc fact-checking. People intuitively felt the problem was happening to them in the moment, not something that better training would solve.
The spreadsheet below contains the raw response data. The graph summarises the distribution across all HMW candidates.

categories.title: Solution
categories.summary: Shield Content Warning — a Chrome extension that detects and flags misinformation in real time.
categories.description: From 1,080 raw ideas down to one working MVP: how we arrived at the Shield Content Warning extension.

blocks.heading: Problem Statement
paragraphs: Our problem statement focused on Algorithmic Amplification of Misinformation on Social Media. After conducting interviews, creating personas, journey maps, and root cause analysis, we moved into the ideation stage to generate possible solutions.

blocks.heading: Ideation Process
paragraphs: We used the 6-3-5 Brainwriting Method with 10 different How Might We (HMW) questions created from the root causes identified earlier. In this method, 6 members generated 3 ideas every 5 minutes across multiple rounds, producing around 1,080 raw ideas.

From these, we filtered and analysed nearly 900 useful ideas by categorising them based on Feasibility (can it realistically be built?), Viability (can it sustain and function effectively?), and Desirability (would users actually want it?).

We also divided ideas into short-term, medium-term, and long-term solutions. To narrow further, we plotted all ideas on a graph — X-axis: Effort required, Y-axis: Value provided — and focused only on the second quadrant (high value, lower effort). This reduced the set to around 514 strong ideas, which were then grouped into three final solution groups.

blocks.heading: Three Final Solution Groups
paragraphs: Group A: Warning Shield — A browser/Chrome extension that detects suspicious or misleading content and overlays warning indicators directly on webpages.
Group B: Truth Finder — An on-demand fact-checking tool that verifies claims using trusted sources and provides quick debunks.
Group C: Credibility Coach — A gamified media literacy platform that encourages responsible sharing and teaches users how to identify misinformation.

blocks.heading: Why We Chose Group A
paragraphs: Out of the three groups, we selected Group A: Warning Shield because it was the most feasible solution to build within the available time, it works directly during browsing making the experience real-time and practical, it does not require user accounts, backend servers, or paid APIs, it aligns strongly with our core problem of algorithmically amplified misinformation, and it can be developed as a fully working MVP using only HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

blocks.heading: About the Extension
paragraphs: The final solution is a Chrome extension called Shield Content Warning. The extension passively scans webpage content while the user browses and identifies suspicious patterns such as urgency manipulation, emotional manipulation, unverified claims, conspiracy framing, misleading statistics, and obscured sources.

When suspicious content is detected, the extension highlights it with warning indicators, displays contextual explanations, blurs high-severity misinformation, shows credibility information through overlays, and provides quick access to fact-checking resources.
The extension also includes sensitivity controls, site whitelisting, dismiss functionality, and a popup dashboard showing flagged content count.

blocks.heading: How the Extension Works
paragraphs: The extension scans visible text on webpages using a content script and compares it against predefined misinformation signal patterns stored locally. Matching content is assigned a severity score — Low, Medium, or High — and suspicious sections are visually flagged using overlays and badges.

High-severity content is blurred until the user chooses to reveal it. A popup dashboard summarises all flagged passages on the current page. The entire system runs locally in the browser without requiring external APIs or servers.

blocks.heading: Working Demo
paragraphs: The screenshots below show Shield running live on real websites. In the first, Shield has flagged a passage on NDTV with a warning overlay. In the second, Shield has detected urgency language in Google search results and surfaced the signal inline.

items.caption: Shield flagging a suspicious passage on NDTV

items.alt: Shield detecting urgency in Google results
items.caption: Shield detecting urgency language in Google search results

blocks.heading: How to Install
paragraphs: 1. Download the ZIP file using the link below.
2. Extract the ZIP file.
3. Open Google Chrome and go to: chrome://extensions/
4. Enable Developer Mode from the top right corner.
5. Click the Load unpacked button.
6. Select the extracted extension folder containing the manifest.json file.
The extension will now be installed and ready to use.

categories.title: Transcripts
categories.description: Verbatim transcripts of every recording in the Interviews archive.

blocks.heading: About the Transcripts
paragraphs: Every recording in the Interviews section has a corresponding written transcript here. These were produced by team members listening back through each session — not auto-generated — so they include the pauses, the corrections, the digressions, and the moments where a respondent changed their mind mid-sentence.

We kept the transcripts verbatim rather than cleaning them up because the texture of how people talk about misinformation turned out to be as informative as what they said. The uncertainty in someone's voice when they try to explain why they shared something, or the frustration in how they describe trying to verify a claim, comes through in the raw transcript in a way that a polished summary would lose.
Select a person below to download their transcript.
